title: You don't reach a habit. You become it.
body: "I am someone who walks every morning" works. "I'm trying to walk more" doesn't. The identity version makes the behaviour an expression of who you are; the goal version makes it a chore you're performing for someone (often yourself).
reflectionQuestion: What habit would you like — and how would you say it as an identity statement?

title: The tiniest version of the habit is the one that survives.
body: Two push-ups, one minute of reading, one breath before email. The tiny version sounds beneath you; that's exactly why it works — it never fails on bad days. Tiny that lasts beats ambitious that breaks.
reflectionQuestion: What's the smallest possible version of a habit you've been trying to build?

title: Every habit has a cue, a routine, and a reward.
body: You can't just want a habit. You need a trigger to start it and something that closes the loop. Brushing your teeth has the bedtime cue and the clean-mouth reward. Building a habit means engineering the cue and noticing the reward, not just sheer will.
reflectionQuestion: What cue could you attach to the habit you want? What's the reward at the end?

title: Willpower is unreliable. Environment is patient.
body: Putting the running shoes by the bed beats deciding to run when you're already in bed. Putting fruit at eye level and biscuits in the cupboard you can't reach beats deciding what to snack on. The environment makes the decision before you do.
reflectionQuestion: What one environment change would make tomorrow's right choice easier?

title: How you recover from a missed day defines the habit.
body: Missing once is a glitch. Missing twice is the start of a new pattern. The line that holds habits together is the rule: never miss twice. After a miss, return — without self-flagellation, without making it a referendum on who you are.
reflectionQuestion: What's your usual response when you miss a day — and how could it be more useful?

title: Self-criticism is a tax on behaviour change.
body: Beating yourself up after a slip doesn't make tomorrow more likely to go well — it makes it less likely. The data is unambiguous. The kinder internal voice predicts better adherence than the harsh one, every time.
reflectionQuestion: What kind of voice would actually help you get back on track tomorrow?

title: New habit + existing habit = stickier than either alone.
body: "After I pour my morning coffee, I write down three things I'm focusing on today." The existing habit (coffee) is the cue for the new one (focusing). You don't have to remember the new habit — it rides on the old one.
reflectionQuestion: What existing morning or evening routine could you attach a new tiny habit to?

title: Subtraction is undervalued. Most lives need less, not more.
body: The habit you most need to build might be the habit you most need to stop. Cutting one corrosive habit often outperforms adding three good ones — and frees the bandwidth for the next move.
reflectionQuestion: What's the one habit you'd subtract if you could only pick one?

title: Discipline strains. Devotion sustains.
body: Things you do out of grim duty rarely last. Things you do because they're part of how you want to live — they last. The same activity can be either, depending on the story you tell yourself about it.
reflectionQuestion: Which of your habits is currently powered by discipline that would last longer as devotion?

title: You are always becoming someone. Pick who.
body: Every habit is a vote for a version of you. Each repetition reinforces an identity. The question isn't "how do I become disciplined?" — it's "who am I voting for with today's behaviour?"
reflectionQuestion: If your habits this week were a vote, who did they vote for?

title: Reduce friction for good. Add friction for bad.
body: Good habits: lay out clothes, prep the meal, put the book on the pillow. Bad habits: take the app off the home screen, leave the bottle at the shop, unsubscribe from the newsletter. Two-second changes that compound.
reflectionQuestion: What's one piece of friction you could add to a habit you want to do less of?

title: Real change is boring while it's happening.
body: The version of you a year from now isn't built by the breakthrough — it's built by the unremarkable Tuesday afternoons where you did the small thing. Most of self-development is showing up on days when it doesn't feel like anything.
reflectionQuestion: What's a small habit you've sustained for months that feels boring but is doing real work?
